segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Listen to a talk on a history podcast.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: When you think of 19th century inventors, what comes to mind?

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Maybe, like most people, you think of an inventor alone in his laboratory, tinkering with devices that no one else had ever dreamed of.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Well, actually, there were often multiple people working on similar inventions at once, they'd improve on each other's work, compete or collaborate.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Have you heard of Louis Latimer, who invented one kind of early air conditioner and improved the toilet systems in trains?

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Latimer, as a black man, was excluded from many opportunities that his White peers enjoyed in the United States.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: He never even got to attend high school, but he taught himself, practiced on his own, to be a drafter-someone who makes technical drawings.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Latimer's drawings were so skillful, they were practically works of art.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: He wrote poetry, and you can see his poetic sensibility even in his drafting work.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: After practicing to learn drafting, Latimer worked with several famous inventors over the span of his career.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Working with Hiram Maxim, Latimer developed and patented a major innovation in carbon filaments.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: A carbon filament is a thin wire inside a light bulb that glows with electricity to produce light.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: Latimer made these filaments stronger and longer lasting and easier to make.

segments.speaker: Speaker
segments.text: This was a major step in making lighting feasible and affordable for the general public.
